title: Dan Loeb: The Lost Art of Short Selling, and Why Stock Picking is Back

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Legendary activist investor, Dan Loeb, he of course is the CEO and CIO of Third Point.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: The lost art of short selling has come back and it's absolutely critical.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Doesn't matter what you do, you have to be really selective. People talk about stock pickers' market, this is a bond and credit pickers' market.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: When we were small, our main tool was shame and humor.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Dan Loeb turning up the heat on Nestle over the weekend.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: The shift has really been much more towards a dare to be great message. Activism without proxy contests is like Catholicism without hell.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You're very active on the Twitter as well. You've found your voice.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Oh, well. A lot of emotion brewing there.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Can we actually start with that? Before Twitter, you were actually quite active, but they were in very different places. I mean, you were in Wall Street Bets before Wall Street Bets existed. Can you just walk us through your evolution as a public persona?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Sure. I mean, there was this brand new technology that came out called the internet. And really shortly thereafter, long before Reddit or any of these other things, there was a series of chat boards. There was, you know, Yahoo, there was something called Silicon Investor, a few other ones. And people would congregate and kibitz. It was done mostly anonymously, and it was an interesting place to exchange ideas. It was really the Wild West. People could pretty much say or do anything, but there was a lot of substance there too. It's not actually that much different from today.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You... did you engage at all in any trolling per se?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Well, some people use the term OG, sometimes I say I was the OT.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The original troll.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no, I did. I mean, it was fun. And, you know, I didn't know I was one day going to run institutional money and have a big fund, and, you know, I was just having fun and blowing off steam and, and, yeah, it was fun. I mean, investing is fun, and particularly on the short side. I mean, there's so much humor in it when you detect these companies, especially in the '90s. I mean, it was really unsupervised. There were some incredibly fraudulent companies out there, and it was just fun to uncover them and kind of taunt the management teams and ultimately prevail.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You have one story above others that kind of stands out in that era?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: I mean, there were, there were a bunch. There was, wow. There was a company called Actrade that I remember run by a guy who was like a repeat fraudster, and we uncovered it and, you know, I know we really got under this person's skin and ultimately... it was really just a factoring company trading at five, six... I don't remember what it was... some...

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: large multiple of book value and they had created a new technology called TADS. I don't remember what TADS stood for, but they were basically repackaging factor securities and saying that they had some special technology, they were financing refrigerators and things like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Tell us, um, your evolution as an investor. When you started Third Point, I mean, you started with very, very little capital, now it's almost 30 billion of AUM. You're multi-strat. But you learned at Jefferies, I think, like you learned helping people like David Tepper allocate capital. Just walk us through how you learned to invest.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Well, I, I started, um, really fascinated by investing and wanting to do it. I think when I, I remember when I was 10 years old, my dad took me... my dad was a notoriously bad investor himself. So he didn't give me any good examples. He was a great lawyer, not a great investor. But he took me to meet a broker and I started investing and then in high school, in the 11th grade, I got a job at the branch office of Bear Sterns-- sorry, of PaineWebber, working for a guy named Alan Crown, who let me post his books and make cold calls. And I think we broke certain securities laws, but I think the statute of limitations has passed. I would trade, um, options on Occidental Petroleum and Teledyne. There was a lot of volatility and, um, I think I had flurries of making money and lost all of it a couple of different times, but it was a good, good lesson. I continued doing it in college and then, um, my learning started really formally at Warburg Pincus, where I really learned to value enterprises. That was my first job. In private, kind of a cross the spectrum of private equity and venture capital. I worked at a risk arb firm, which was really invaluable. And then, um, skipping forward, I had, I had way too many jobs in my 20s. But I got really serious at Jefferies. I had a amazing opportunity to work on the distressed debt desk there. I started out as a research analyst and it was just like drinking out of a firehose. There was so much activity, the securities were so cheap coming out of distressed. And, um, it was, you know, the 10,000 hours, 10,000 reps. We would write up, um, different things every, every day. There were big blocks of debt to move. And I really got... that was my real learning point. And, you know, I stress this to people that, you know, everyone's kind of sees mentorship as this sort of hierarchical thing where you, you know, learn from some wise older person, but it's, I, I learned a ton from my colleagues, from my own cohort, and I learned a ton from my customers. You know, like David... Eric Mindich was a, um, boy wonder at Goldman, he was the youngest partner...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Youngest partner at Goldman.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Ran the arb desk there and he had this...

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Triumvirate or quadrumvirate, whatever the four people, I don't want to leave them out, but Amos, Marone, Dinakar, and I can't think... some other guys. Anyway, they were great and they really kind of brought me into their thought process, thinking about event-driven investing. And then, you know, I covered some of the smartest people in the business, including David Tepper. I got to watch their thought process. And I was like a, you know, like a Chinese corporation that was like copying and reverse engineering and taking everything in and creating my database of knowledge and my own operating system, kind of taking the best out of what all these different people did.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And what was that style when you first started Third Point? What did you... what was that expression?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: That was, well, I think, you know, we call it event-driven investing. It was really less focused on the quality of business, more focused on very complex transactions: takeovers, spin-offs, risk-arbitrage, bankruptcies, privatizations, demutualizations. And these transactions created unbelievable opportunities for alpha because of the confluence of dislocation, opacity, kind of time, but also—and this goes and nothing changes, I always quote this Jesse Livermore line, 'there's nothing new under the sun'—a real focus on management incentives. So in all these different kinds of transactions, management was incentivized to sandbag their numbers during a time when there was an excess supply of securities, where their options were being set, and we as co-investors got to come in with these depressed projections and ride along not just the... well, we got to ride along a few different things that would happen: greater transparency and understanding of the business, coverage, companies that delivered a top line and margins and ROE and everything else better than expectations. So it was really a golden era for that type of investing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And from where that started to what Third Point is today, just describe that and where you want to, like, where do you go from here?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so stylistically that event approach, it's still something we think about, it's in our framework. But I think what happened, really when technology became a bigger force, but really everything changed, is a greater focus on business quality and innovation and disruption and more thematic on the one hand, understanding of consumer trends, what's going on in financial services, what's the economic macro backdrop that's supporting all this, and of course the big topic of this event, you know, AI is sort of the culmination of that.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: But all of these major technological innovations that have really happened since you... You could make money before by not being technology savvy in the markets. You could be technologically illiterate or just say I don't do it, and you could also be even more or less ec- you know, up until the GFC I think you could be more or less economically illiterate and make a lot of money.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And now?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you wouldn't want to be either one of those things. I mean, given how much, how much more important...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Like the tech throughline needs to be understood everywhere.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But even if you're like Blue Owl and you're trade- I mean Blue Owl obviously is very sophisticated in tech now, but any pool of capital that used to not be correlated is effectively correlated?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: I mean yes, yeah you could say that. And I just want to answer your question just to kind of fast forward and give people a snapshot of what we do today. Rob Schwartz is my partner and we took Kempo Karate together when we were 10 years old. He was a purple belt, I think I never made it past yellow belt, but we reconnected at our 20-year reunion in- I'm aging both of us, sorry to give up your secret Rob- in 1999 was our 20-year reunion, and he was working as a sales rep for wireless RF components. I said, 'Wow, this guy would be great to do channel checks for us.' And then I asked him a couple years later, 'Hey, you meet some smart people, have you ever come across a really savvy engineer? We should invest.' We didn't know what we were doing, we weren't venture capitalists, but we were getting behind a person. There was a guy named Dave Fisher, started a company called Radia Communications, they made chips, he made chips that were, I still remember, ABG compatible for WiFi base stations, and ultimately the company was sold to Texas Instruments. And you know, we've- I won't go deep into our venture business, but that we started to do within the fund. We've done a couple of dedicated funds, so we have that strand of activity. We can talk about a little bit more about what we're thinking and how we're seeing this, but I think what ultimately I want you to get to is that all these things are interconnected and come together under the platform that we have today because we have the main hedge fund which does credit, equity long-short, the credit is both structured credit and high yield. We have a CLO business that we acquired. We started a- a private credit business that does traditional private credit, direct- sponsor financing, direct lending, and workouts, which is very important. So credit solutions as they call it, a lot to do there. And then we started an insurance company a few years ago. It's not the first insurance company we did, we did a P&C company but this one is was wholly owned, now we own half of it. And the insurance company...

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: captures basically the investment grid part of what we do. So private credit through structured vehicles, structured credit, whole loans, investment grade both private and public, but we also can use our surplus capital in very interesting ways. So...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So what's the role of the human? What's the role of Dan Loeb in running Third Point 10 years from now? Like 10 years before Dan Loeb was 100% of Third Point.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Uh-huh.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And then there's now there's agents, there's AI, there's all this learning, there's all of this data. Where do you see the role of the human and where do you see the role of systems making decisions, allocating capital, managing risk?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: I mean it's first of all, investing now like, well first of all my time is spent primarily on managing the hedge fund which for now is the biggest capital pool, the most important business that we're in.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: The human element, I think this is true for everyone you have here, like the element of the social component, the human network of knowing people, being able to capture opportunities, work with people, interact, like that's never going away. Like you're never gonna, maybe you can theorize that there will be agents that will sit at Andreessen Horowitz and whoever else, your funds, but I think the human will always have to be there because people like to, you know...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They want to know who's making or losing the money.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and there's a thing that I think the agents, the AI will never really be able to look in your eye and assess all the things that you've...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You've expanded your philosophy of investing in companies from cheap cattle, cheap securities with catalysts, as I think how you described it on a podcast recently, and now you're very concerned about moats, defensibility, and just the quality or the brittleness as Chamath likes to remind us of the revenue. So maybe could you tell us how you evolved that core thinking about the quality of companies and then maybe give us some examples of the companies that now fit through that filter where you feel they have a moat, you feel they have durability.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, obviously that's everything right now. Chamath talks about the time-bounded value of companies and I think that's essential.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What are the companies that are going to be around 7 to 10 to 20... what are the real moats that exist out there?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: And it's it's it is harder now. I don't think we can I don't know that we can really go out you know 10 or 20 years ago. By the way I think we deluded ourselves earlier because I think if you ask people about the moat around you know IBM or you know some of the other companies that were...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: AOL.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: AOL.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, who, you know, you say the same thing. I mean, look, we're investing outside of tech into companies that have some great... well, first of all, it also comes back to the management because we can't really just look at a product or technology and say, oh, this is going to be it forever. So we're really looking for a management team that we think will be adaptable. And just like you guys were saying last night, you don't want to be on boards of companies. These are things that they should be doing. So I think that's a huge part of it, like finding management teams that you really believe in that have a proven ability to stay ahead of...

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Is that quantifiable or is it still very much a subjective...

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Sorry, is what?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Is it quantifiable, assessing the management team? Have you built a rubric for doing that? No, it's still a very subjective, qualitative...

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: I think it's one of those things after 30 years there's like a pattern recognition and you...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Let me ask a question on screening, you know, I think you've said recently publicly that there's a lot of opportunities on the short side in the market right now for the first time in a long time. How do you start top-down, is that a top-down or is it an opportunistic, you know, something comes across the wire and you guys jump on it in kind of an event-driven way? Or do you guys have kind of a systematic top-down approach to looking at the market and finding those opportunities?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yeah, there's no one approach to it. I think one thing that we've avoided is kind of a valuation... a solely valuation-based approach. There's a lot... I've just seen too many people get run over by shorts that have dumb valuations, but they get captured on Reddit or one of these other things and they just get their, you know... or like some of these space companies right now that there's no rhyme or reason. We had a really strong view on homebuilders from last year that there were two things going on. It wasn't just rates, mortgage spreads that were depressing housing prices. That home prices... that the homebuilding industry was first structurally impaired because of the way that they were all pretending to be NVR, which is to say all pretending to be asset light, but they had massive commitments to these land pools, which in things that they said were options, but they were really very committed in the capital. And that that value was going on... but that the homebuilding industry was really the last industry that had this post-COVID hangover of inventory disruptions and pricing that really made no sense. You had all those prices...

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: gone up at unsustainable levels, but so did building costs went up and and buyers are no longer able to pay those prices at the current current in the current financing environment, but that the but they've also gotten squeezed by by inflation and costs. So that's been, you know, something so we've been short things related to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Let me bring Sacks into the discussion here. Sacks, we've learned a little about distribution of public securities, your famous in the All-In theme song of this great quote, let your winners ride. I'm curious when you hear Dan talking about this, how you think about as a private market investor how to navigate distributing equities and how you've sharpened your blade about, you know, which ones have brittle or, you know, more robust revenue.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I mean, that's I'm sure you guys share this. It's one of the most vexing questions. We were private investors in Palantir, and I think we sold all our stock in the 20s. Huge mistake.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Gosh, I've missed a 10x after going public. Or an 8x or something.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: We were private. We led the B-round in in Upstart.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Mm-hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: That was one. I think we learned not to go on boards anymore because it restricts your ability to be liquid. But we're also early investors in Enphase.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: And we sold some stock on the IPO and then took a tax hit. And then I think sold it under a dollar. And the stock, I think, had we stayed on would have made 4 billion dollars. So I'm not claiming to have any great expertise in knowing how to best distribute our...

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Markets are brutal.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's so hard. I mean, your Sacks... this is why I bring it up. We've all struggled with this. Sacks, where do you... where have you wound up?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I I think it's case by case. I mean, there's some companies where like I was on a board and you can't sell and you end up regretting that. And then there's others where the best thing to do is just hold on to that stock forever.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Examples. In your portfolio where you've made great decisions.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: We're not going to talk about the ones that didn't do so well, but but no, I mean, look, I've owned Meta and Palantir as a private, you know, as a venture investor, as an angel investor.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And you sell...?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Well, I sold some and held on to some. Obviously, in hindsight, you take Meta. I think Meta IPO'd as Facebook back then. IPO'd at a 50 billion dollar valuation.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Yeah, 50 billion. And then it went down to 18. Now it's...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 800, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Can you imagine how alternate universe if Chamath never sold his Facebook, how insufferable he'd be? Or if Friedberg never sold his Google? Friedberg would be worth 10 billion.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: No, no, I'd be... I wouldn't be nearly as good.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: What's that?

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: I wouldn't be nearly as good like an analyst because it created tension... created...

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Not real. It's not. It's not earned. So back in those days...

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: 10 years ago we thought 100 billion dollar market cap company was pretty much as big as anything could get.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: And so Facebook at 50 or whatever, it's like the upside was to a 100. And things are just totally different now. We have multi-trillion dollar companies, the market's so much bigger and that changes everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I mean that's a rub against Nvidia, which is a five billion dollar company and people feel like it's a sort of a ceiling on it. I think we'll look back at some point in time and say that was a foolish way to think about Nvidia given its dominant position and its valuation relative to everyone else today.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Is it undervalued right now?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Yes, absolutely on earnings over the next two or three years. It is.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And is it because people are having a hard time processing the largest entity that's ever existed in human history?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I think that and the narrative that the—well, first of all, technically there's all this other stuff that's growing faster and going up more. People are—the long-short pods are structured such that they have to be short something. So Nvidia feels like a safe short. By the way, Google was a safe short, Amazon was a safe short. So, I mean, this just happens and sometimes they'll languish at a valuation and then they break out. I think that'll eventually happen with Nvidia.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: But there's probably some boundary condition discount to that, right? Like we've never seen a valuation like this, you can't over-bet that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I want to shift topics for a second. I just want to talk society and culture before we run out of time with you. There was this incredible thing that you told me which I want to relate to these guys, which is you're very passionate about criminal justice reform, and specifically you were a key person to get the pardon of Ross Ulbricht. Tell us your views on criminal justice, why it hit such a nerve, and then why Ross Ulbricht? What was—what happened there that said I must fight for this guy?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Well let me take a step back and just talk about my framework for philanthropy, which is I think not unlike Brad Gerstner and many people in the room here, is that I care—I would say everybody up here—I care deeply about income inequality, I care deeply about making sure that as many people have opportunities to the incredible things that we've all had here. So my interest in criminal justice reform really started earlier with an interest in education and education reform. And I was very lucky to get on the board—to start supporting and get on the board and ultimately be chairman of Success Academies, which is a charter school network in New York. And I do think nobody talks about it, but the thing that's hiding out in plain sight for everybody is that the problems with income inequality isn't that, you know, Jeff Bezos is going to be a trillionaire or all these other people are gaining wealth. It's that we're not equipping children and particularly the most vulnerable children with the intellectual tools—

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: tools that they need to succeed and compete. And it's not because poverty is this intractable thing that can't be overcome. We've proven that it can be. The problem is that the unions and the basic principles that we all use in business, which is accountability and merit and cultivating talent, is set aside for the benefit of adults who are part of these unions. And it's a systemic thing. It's not a lack of money. It's really a lack of—it's just a broken structure.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Accountability is I think what I'm hearing, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: So I spent a lot of time on that, just leave it at that. Um, I then became aware, and it's interesting, I was looking for issues that conservatives—you know, it's great to see Fetterman and McCormick up here—like, what are issues that conservatives and liberals, progressives can agree on? Hopefully they can agree that we want young people to be better educated. I think we can also agree that whenever you put the government in charge of something, they'll fuck it up one way or another. I want to give you guys a shout-out, though, for not fucking up this private-public partnership with the investments in the private sector, because I think this administration's done an enormously good job at backing companies. Let's put that aside. It's one of the rare instances where I've seen that. But—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And can you give an example of that that's standing out in your mind?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: We have a company in our portfolio called Atom Computing that, with many other quantum companies, has gotten money from the government. And we were just super impressed that they—how they contracted with us to engage with them in cryptography and to meet the government's needs. But also in the financial component, they drove a really tough bargain. The—the government, the taxpayers are going to make a ton of money on this. And their involvement also has—will contribute meaningfully to the value of this business. It's just like a win all the way around.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So they're an investor and a customer.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Right. And they are capturing part of that value as a customer for the American people, which I think everybody deserves.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, so back to the—

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: So criminal justice reform. First of all, there's a lot of bad people in jail. I'm not one of—you know, I think the criminal justice movement has been undermined by folks who see it as an opportunity to not prosecute, not deal with bad people that are out there. But there's also a lot of people that are rehabilitated. Uh, well, there's really three different categories. There's people who are falsely convicted, there are people who have shown contrition and rehabilitation, and then there are those who just had a really disproportionate sentence relative to what they did. There's a case right now of a guy named Jonathan Grobman, who was a Dalton—Grobman...

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: orchid diapers and formula, got an 18-year sentence for dealing these goods. In the case of Ross Ulbricht, I was approached by someone and this just seemed... Ross, as people may know, probably this room knows, he was sort of a folk hero because he had this sort of cat and mouse game with the government. He ran Silk Road. Silk Road was a one of the first like crypto-based exchanges. He acknowledges that he did things that were illegal that he shouldn't have done, he regrets it. Drugs were dealt on the exchange, but that's what he was accused of. The government later said that there were murder-for-hire incidents. That was never... he was never prosecuted for that and he denies that that ever happened. But in any case, he was sentenced to double life plus 40 years. Who knows how he got the extra 40 years on there and how he would spend it after he'd been there for two lifetimes. And there was a woman I met through Intel named Rivetaz who alerted me to this, friends with Olaf Carlson-Wee and sort of the crypto insiders. And I thought about it, I'm like, this guy's got no way out. There's no recourse through the system to get someone with a life sentence out of jail. This will only work with a presidential pardon. And we worked on it, we had some familiarity with the pardon process. Worked on it, then I approached Charlie Kirk about this. And Charlie really embraced this and embraced this individual as someone who had been falsely, or not falsely, but unfairly sentenced. He took it to the president. Charlie had a also had an attorney named David Warrington who's currently the...

President Trump signed an executive order on Tuesday that asks technology companies to voluntarily give the government oversight of new AI models before releasing them to the public. There is a big change here from an earlier version that got scrapped a couple of weeks ago, which is that the number of days that the government will get to review models is down from 90 days to 30. And apparently that was enough for former White House AI Tsar David Sacks to give it his blessing. Chamath, what do you make of the Trump EO?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Okay, what is going on here? Because when we talked to Sundar Pichai a couple weeks ago, he was going to the White House to, um, to be there for this EO signing. But then that got delayed because apparently David Sacks had a fit and didn't like something that was in the order. So now we have this 30-day provision. Um, I am just very confused about the state of the AI order and whether David Sacks is still kind of running the show from the shadows over there.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, he's not the AI tsar anymore, but he definitely still has sway. And there are some within the administration who believe that if the government is able to hold up the release of a new model for 90 days, that could hurt American competitiveness. If you bring it down to 30 days, there's just not as concerned. So that's the change that was made. I mean, the part that just makes me roll my eyes at all of this is just that the fact that all of this is still voluntary. Like, I think we are now past the point where frontier models actually should actually have to submit to mandatory required testing before they inflict these models on the public, but we're still not there yet.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Wait, so it's voluntary to do the 30 days?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's voluntary to submit it, and then the government has 30 days to, I guess, you know, give you notes.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I see. I'm still confused about the state of AI regulation in this country, and my going assumption is that until I see something that is, you know, passed into law and signed, we are just sort of operating in the vibes universe.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, the state of AI regulation in this country is vibes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: AI regulation is hey, what do you guys want to do? What sounds good to you? What would work for you?

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Stop generating!

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right. What's next?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, we've got... US is said to be investigating George Santos. Haven't heard that name in years. Over Kalshi betting. This one comes to us from our colleagues at the New York Times. Federal authorities are investigating whether former US representative George Santos engaged in insider trading by betting on a prediction market about whether he would show up at President Trump's State of the Union address in late February. And Kalshi has referred this matter to the Justice Department and the CFTC for further investigation.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: So say a bit more because am I right that there were maybe some indications that he was going to go to the State of the Union, but then it appears that he may have become aware of that and then gone and placed a bet that he would not go and then did not go?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes, so the pattern of facts as we understand them is that just before the State of the Union, George Santos goes on social media and says that he's going to attend. But he missed the speech and around the time of the event, Kalshi detected that he had bet against his own attendance. Um, so we salute a legend.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: This diva truly will go down in history. George Santos, ice-cold. Now look, there's a very narrow category of crimes, Kevin, for which I think you should not get in trouble, and this is absolutely one of them. If you are out there on social media engaging in trickery to get people to lose money in a prediction market, that I actually think should not be a crime. Or at least, at the very least, I think you should get one for free.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes, very funny. It's always the ones you most expect. And, uh, this is going to be just a hilarious genre of like silly crime stories over the next few years is just increasingly famous people just getting caught with their pants down betting on prediction markets about events that they themselves control.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I will say as an aging millennial, it is very appealing to me the idea that I could profit from saying I was going to go to a party and then not go. That's we've all had that dream.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We've been doing that for free for years. What's wrong with us? Okay, stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: All right, this next one comes to us from 404 Media. Hackers simply asked Meta AI to give them access to high-profile Instagram accounts, and it worked. Hackers say they used Meta AI's supporter chatbot to break into a host of high-profile Instagram profiles by asking the support bot to change the email address associated with the target account. The claims coincide with a series of high-profile Instagram account takeovers, including the Barack Obama White House account, the Chief Master Sergeant of Space Force's account, and Sephora's account. Kevin, what do you make of the fact that you can just apparently access someone else's Instagram account by asking Meta AI?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, I just want to say my old pal at Meta AI...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Nancy would never have done this. She would have upheld the integrity of these Instagram accounts. But now they've turned it over to this crazy chatbot who's just giving away people's passwords.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Look, I know it... I know it seems like this story is bad for Meta, but I actually think it's good because we have finally found something that Meta AI is good for. And I'm not sure that I could name a second thing. So congratulations to the super intelligence laboratory.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I learned about this when someone posted the apparent account credentials and cell phone number of Mark Zuckerberg on X. So, I have not tried the number yet to verify that it works, but it appears that you can just go on and ask Meta AI for anything.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I'm going to guess that that's not actually his contact information and is in fact some sort of fishing scam that would, you know, pwn you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Only one way to find out. Everyone try calling Mark Zuckerberg at...

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Kevin, you know we have a strict no-doxxing policy here on the show.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, well I'll save that for our bonus content. All right, stop generating. Next up, United fl... oh this, this is my favorite story of the week.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: United flight forced to turn around because of a Bluetooth speaker name. That's the Verge headline. A United Airlines flight from Newark to Mallorca, Spain, last Saturday night had to turn around about two hours after takeoff and do an emergency landing due to security concerns over a Bluetooth signal. The crew on the flight asked for passengers to turn off their Bluetooth devices multiple times, and everyone complied except for one speaker that belonged to a 16-year-old boy and was named 'Bomb'.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's pretty funny because it's like there's maybe only one word in the English language that you could name your Bluetooth speaker that would force an emergency landing, and you picked it, brother. Congrats.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: It's so true. I was following the story on Reddit where there were, like, weirdly a number of passengers on this flight were like active on Reddit. And so during this they were like, the pilots and the... are coming on and telling us that we all have to turn off our Bluetooth devices immediately. What's going on? And then you kind of follow in real time. And finally they figured out, yeah, there's this Bluetooth speaker and when you connect it, it shows up on the little list of Bluetooth devices as 'Bomb', which is a very bad name for a Bluetooth speaker. Don't do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's a very bad name for a Bluetooth speaker unless you plan on playing some bomb ass tunes. You know? Look, it's all about context. Also, I like... I have news for people who are, you know, I don't know, running airport security: most bombs that would blow up planes you cannot actually connect to them via Bluetooth and are not named 'Bomb' in the Bluetooth list. These are not discoverable devices that are advertising themselves as what they are.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: It reminds me of like, do you remember when people used to give their like home Wi-Fi networks like names like, you know, 'CIA surveillance van' or something? It's like... the real CIA surveillance van is not named that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Look, I think we have a very... important message to deliver to airline security and that is you guys gotta have a sense of humor. You know what I mean?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: You guys need to relax, live a little.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I can tell you, Phil.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: It was a damn Bluetooth speaker. Do you know how irritated I would be if I was two hours into my flight into Mallorca and now we had to turn around?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Trying to get to that beach.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, last one out of the hat.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: You didn't say stop generating.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Stop generating. Okay, last one out of the hat. Oh, Casey, this one's in your wheelhouse. Survivor boss Jeff Probst says Kalshi and Polymarket are, quote, 'incentivizing people to lie, cheat and steal.' This one comes to us from Variety. Apparently there was some drama on Survivor recently when one of the episodes was spoiled due to widely circulated reports about the odds on these prediction markets, Kalshi and Polymarket. On both platforms, Aubry Bracco was forecast to have an above 80% chance of winning before the season even premiered.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so um, this is a story that brings together two of my favorite things, which are Survivor and hating on prediction markets. You know, we have talked on the show about the fact that one of the main things that prediction markets do is incentivize you to betray your friends, family, coworkers, and possibly your country. And so what Jeff Probst is noticing is that now, like all of his crew members could stand to make a ton of money, you know, by betting on one of these prediction markets. Now, it is important to say that it does... we we do not currently have information to suggest that this is what happened. Like we don't know if somebody in particular on the Survivor crew who may have leaked the information, but now we're just living in a world where everyone is suspicious and uh, you know, I mean I think it just contributes to the low-trust society that we're already living in. So this is obviously very bad, but let me take this opportunity to say, Kevin, that while I do think that Aubry was uh played a great game and had a great season, I have to give a shout out to the greatest player to never win the game, Cirie Fields, who truly came so close on this season and was amazing to watch every single week and I was absolutely crushed when she lost. Um, so uh just incredible work, Cirie, and uh incredible work to the to the Survivor team for putting out 50 really amazing seasons of television.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Only like one or two of which you could bet on on prediction markets. I mean, they were just doing this for the love of the game.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Mm-hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Uh, we have to start pretty soon like a segment that's just about people getting caught for doing stupid stuff on prediction markets. Because in addition to the George Santos thing and the Survivor thing, there was also a story this week about a Google engineer who was charged with using inside information to make a million dollars on Polymarket by placing bets on allegedly what users were searching for. Um, so truly no corner of society is safe from the corrupting influence of prediction markets.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Yeah, when you've got that Google money and you're still trying to make a little extra scratch by like corrupting a prediction market, something's gone wrong in this society.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So, uh, that is Hat GPT. Let's close the hat.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Close up the hat.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Welcome back everybody. It's the All-In podcast. Episode 125. We have a very special guest today. He is the founder and CEO of Third Point. He's one of the most successful hedge fund managers in history.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: He's also written some of the most famous letters in finance history. Please welcome to the show, Dan Loeb.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Thanks, Jason. Great to be here. Good to see the whole crew.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Welcome, Dan.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Hey, Dan.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Good to see you, Dan.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And we've got the whole crew here. We've got the rain man, David Friedberg.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Hey everyone.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The dictator, David Sacks.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Great to be here.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And the big kahuna, Chamath Palihapitiya.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: What's up, boys?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Dan, you know these guys. You've played poker with them. You've invested with them. You've probably fought with some of them over the years.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: All of the above. It's been a long time.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So let's just jump right in. There's so much going on in the markets. We had the banking crisis, inflation, interest rates. Dan, what is your top-down view of the world right now?

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: Well, I think we're in a period where macro matters more than it has in a long time. For the last decade, you could almost ignore the macro and just focus on growth and tech. But now with the cost of capital going up, you really have to be disciplined.

full_transcript.speaker: Dan Loeb
full_transcript.text: We're seeing a lot of companies that were market darlings a year or two ago really struggle with this new reality. It's a regime change. We're moving from a period of easy money to one where capital has a real cost.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: And do you think this is a long-term structural shift, or is this just a blip while we work through the inflation numbers?

highlights: Activist investor Dan Loeb describes a shift in his strategy from using "shame and humor" to a "dare to be great" message, emphasizing that technological literacy is now a requirement for successful investing in any sector.

While AI is increasingly capable of solving complex technical problems, the human element remains essential in finance for building networks and performing the subjective "pattern recognition" needed to assess a management team's adaptability.

The "Leiden Declaration" reflects a growing concern among mathematicians that AI-generated proofs could overwhelm the field with "slop," potentially devaluing the human creativity and rigorous discovery that have historically defined the discipline.

Discussions on philanthropy highlight a focus on systemic accountability in education and criminal justice, specifically advocating for the use of presidential pardons in cases of disproportionate sentencing.

Experts note that while AI models have achieved "gold medal" status in high school level math competitions, the jump to frontier research math remains a significant challenge that will require new methods of logical reasoning.
summary:
This transcript features an extensive conversation with Dan Loeb, CEO of Third Point, covering his professional evolution from an early internet "troll" and short-seller to a sophisticated activist investor. Loeb details his transition from traditional "event-driven" investing—which focused on corporate catalysts like bankruptcies and spin-offs—to a modern strategy centered on technological innovation, business "moats," and high-quality management. A recurring theme is the current "regime change" in the markets; Loeb and the hosts argue that as the era of "easy money" ends, investors must shift from purely chasing growth to exercising discipline regarding the cost of capital and macroeconomic trends.

The discussion also delves into Loeb’s philanthropic focus on education and criminal justice reform, most notably his role in securing the pardon of Silk Road founder Ross Ulbricht. This leads into a deep dive into the impact of AI on advanced mathematics. The participants highlight a growing divide in the mathematical community: while some see AI as a "jetpack" for human research, others—represented by the "Leiden Declaration"—worry that AI-generated proofs may undermine the human integrity of the field. This tension reflects a broader societal anxiety about AI’s potential to "crowd out" human expertise in disciplines previously thought to be the pinnacle of human thought.

Finally, the transcript concludes with a rapid-fire look at contemporary tech news, including the "vibes-based" nature of voluntary AI regulation and the ethical pitfalls of prediction markets. The hosts critique stories ranging from secret robot testing in Airbnbs to the social engineering of Meta’s AI to bypass security. Throughout these segments, the overarching theme is the disruption of established norms—whether in finance, academia, or law—by emerging technologies and the unpredictable human behaviors they incentivize.
